schools.summary: The seven last plagues: 'and I saw another sign in heaven, great and marvellous, seven angels having the seven last plagues; for in them is filled up the wrath of God' — a great and marvelous sign: seven angels bearing the seven last plagues (introducing the final series of judgments, the seven vials, ch. 16, 'great and marvellous' marking the importance and awe of the vision); 'the seven last plagues' the final series of judgments completing God's wrath ('last' because with them the outpouring of wrath is finished, the climax of the judgments on the antichristian world); 'in them is filled up (etelesthe — completed) the wrath of God' — in these plagues the wrath of God brought to its full and final expression (the measure of God's judgment completed, the seven last plagues consummating the divine wrath against the impenitent) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Victorinus and Bede: the seven angels with the seven last plagues in which the wrath of God is filled up mark the completion and consummation of the divine judgment; in the recapitulatory frame, the vials re-tread and bring to their final term the judgments of the trumpets, the wrath of God reaching its fullness in this last sevenfold outpouring upon the impenitent worshippers of the beast.

themes: another sign in heaven, great and marvellous, seven angels having the seven last plagues
for in them is filled up the wrath of God
the seven angels with the seven last plagues
the final judgments completing and consummating God's wrath

schools.summary: The victors on the sea of glass: 'and I saw as it were a sea of glass mingled with fire: and them that had gotten the victory over the beast, and over his image, and over his mark, and over the number of his name, stand on the sea of glass, having the harps of God' — the sea of glass (4:6, before the throne) now mingled with fire (the fire marking the judgments, or the trials through which the victors passed, or the glory and holiness of God, the calm crystalline sea now shot with fire); those who 'had gotten the victory over the beast, and over his image, and over his mark, and over the number of his name' (ch. 13, overcame the antichristian power by refusing to worship and bear the mark, even unto death) standing triumphant on the sea of glass; 'having the harps of God' given harps for the song of triumph (vv.3-4, the conquerors, like the redeemed, 14:2-3; 5:8, praising God with harps, the victory over the beast issuing in heavenly worship and song) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: a sea of glass mingled with fire
them that had gotten the victory over the beast... stand on the sea of glass, having the harps of God
the victors over the beast standing on the sea of glass
the conquerors of the antichristian power, victorious and worshiping

schools.summary: The song of Moses and the Lamb: 'and they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying, Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints' — the victors singing a song joining 'the song of Moses' (the song of deliverance at the Red Sea, Exodus 15, after God overthrew Pharaoh) and 'the song of the Lamb' (the song of redemption), the deliverance from the beast celebrated as a new and greater Exodus, the two songs one — God's triumph over his enemies and the redemption of his people; 'Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty' the praise of God's great and marvelous works (his mighty acts of judgment and salvation); 'just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints (or of the nations/ages)' the praise of God's justice (his ways in judgment and salvation just and true, the victors vindicating God's righteousness, even the dreadful judgments owned as just and true, God praised as King) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb
Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints
the song of Moses and the Lamb
the new Exodus deliverance and the vindication of God's just judgments

schools.summary: The call to fear and worship the holy God: 'who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for thou only art holy: for all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest' — the victors' song asking who would not fear and glorify God (the rhetorical question affirming that all ought to, and ultimately will, fear and honor him, the universal homage due to God); 'for thou only art holy' the ground (God alone is holy, his unique holiness demanding universal reverence, the song magnifying God's holiness as the ground of worship); 'all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest' the song foreseeing all nations coming to worship God (the universal acknowledgment of his reign, Psalm 86:9), the reason being that God's righteous judgments, now revealed, vindicate him and draw the nations to worship (the manifestation of his justice leading to universal homage) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for thou only art holy
all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest
the song's call to fear and worship the holy God
the universal homage drawn by God's manifest righteous judgments

schools.summary: The opened temple of the testimony: 'and after that I looked, and, behold, the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened' — the heavenly temple, called 'the tabernacle of the testimony' (after the earthly tabernacle that held the ark of the testimony, Exodus 38:21; Numbers 1:50), opened (the source of the coming judgments the very sanctuary of God, the plagues proceeding from God's holy presence); 'the tabernacle of the testimony' recalling the earthly tabernacle containing the testimony (the law, the ark, the dwelling of God's holiness and covenant, the heavenly temple so named marking that the judgments proceed from God's holiness and faithfulness to his covenant and law); the opening showing the coming judgments are from God himself, out of his sanctuary (not arbitrary but proceeding from his holiness and the vindication of his testimony, the plagues God's own appointed acts) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened
the opening of the heavenly temple of the testimony
the final judgments proceeding from God's own sanctuary
the plagues from God's holiness and the vindication of his testimony

schools.summary: The angels in priestly array: 'and the seven angels came out of the temple, having the seven plagues, clothed in pure and white linen, and having their breasts girded with golden girdles' — the seven angels with the plagues coming out of the opened temple, clothed in pure white linen and girded with golden girdles (the priestly, glorious array marking their holy office and the sacredness of their commission); 'pure and white linen' marking the holiness, purity, and glory of the angels (the white linen the dress of priestly, holy service, the agents of God's judgment themselves holy); 'their breasts girded with golden girdles' (like the glorified Christ, 1:13) marking dignity, royalty, and priestly office (the angels of the plagues arrayed as holy, dignified ministers of God's judgment, proceeding from his sanctuary on their solemn errand) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the seven angels came out of the temple, having the seven plagues, clothed in pure and white linen
having their breasts girded with golden girdles
the seven angels emerging in priestly array
the holy, dignified ministers of God's judgment

schools.summary: The golden vials of wrath: 'and one of the four beasts gave unto the seven angels seven golden vials full of the wrath of God, who liveth for ever and ever' — one of the four living creatures (4:6) giving the seven angels seven golden bowls filled with God's wrath (the vials, the vessels of the plagues, handed to the angels for the outpouring, the wrath that of the eternal, living God); 'seven golden vials (phialas — bowls) full of the wrath of God' the vessels from which the seven last plagues will be poured out (ch. 16, golden marking their sacred origin, full of wrath ready to be emptied on the earth, the instruments of the final judgment); 'who liveth for ever and ever' the everliving, eternal God whose wrath is therefore sure and inescapable (the judgments proceeding from the eternal sovereign, the appeal to God's eternity marking the certainty and gravity of the wrath) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: one of the four beasts gave unto the seven angels seven golden vials full of the wrath of God
who liveth for ever and ever
the golden vials of wrath given to the angels
the vessels of the seven last plagues from the eternal God

schools.summary: The temple filled with smoke: 'and the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God, and from his power; and no man was able to enter into the temple, till the seven plagues of the seven angels were fulfilled' — the heavenly temple filled with smoke proceeding from God's glory and power (the smoke, like the cloud that filled the tabernacle and Solomon's temple, Exodus 40:34-35; 1 Kings 8:10-11; Isaiah 6:4, marking the immediate, overwhelming presence and majesty of God, the visible token of his awful presence, glory, and might as the judgments are about to be poured out); 'no man was able to enter into the temple, till the seven plagues of the seven angels were fulfilled' — none able to enter the temple while the judgments proceeded (the closed sanctuary marking that the time of intercession is for the moment suspended, the judgments must run their course, God's awful presence in judgment barring approach until the wrath is fulfilled, the solemnity and finality of the appointed plagues) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: the temple filled with smoke from the glory and power of God, which none could enter until the seven plagues were fulfilled, marks the awful manifestation of God's majesty in judgment — the divine presence so overwhelming that approach is barred while the appointed wrath runs its course, the holiness and power of God displayed in the consummation of his judgments.

themes: the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God, and from his power
no man was able to enter into the temple, till the seven plagues of the seven angels were fulfilled
the temple filled with smoke until the plagues are fulfilled
God's overwhelming presence in judgment barring approach
